perturbation.original_text: Distributor will maintain an adequately trained sales organization, capable of fulfilling its obligation under Subsection 2.2.
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor will endeavor to maintain a sales organization.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the word "adequately trained" and replacing the strong obligation with "endeavor to maintain" introduces ambiguity regarding the level of training required and the obligation to maintain a sales organization, creating a loophole in the contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 17200 prohibits unfair competition. By weakening the requirement for an adequately trained sales organization, the distributor could potentially engage in unfair or deceptive sales practices due to lack of proper training, thus contradicting the spirit and intent of this law. It makes it difficult to prove negligence, as endeavoring is vague and does not hold the same level of legal responsibility as a direct, defined obligation.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 5. Enforcement [17200 - 17210] ( Chapter 5 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 299. ) 17200. As used in this chapter, unfair competition shall mean and include any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice and unfair, deceptive, untrue or misleading advertising and any act prohibited by Chapter 1 (commencing with Section 17500) of Part 3 of Division 7 of the Business and Professions Code. (Amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 430, Sec. 2. Effective January 1, 1993.)

ENFORCEMENT BUSINESS AND PROFESSIONS CODE SECTION 17200-17210 17200. As used in this chapter, unfair competition shall mean and include any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice and unfair, deceptive, untrue or misleading advertising and any act prohibited by Chapter 1 (commencing with Section 17500) of Part 3 of Division 7 of the Business and Professions Code. 17201. As used in this chapter, the term person shall mean and include natural persons, corporations, firms, partnerships, joint stock companies, associations and other organizations of persons. 17201.5. As used in this chapter: (a) "Board within the Department of Consumer Affairs" includes any commission, bureau, division, or other similarly constituted agency within the Department of Consumer Affairs. (b) "Local consumer affairs agency" means and includes any city or county body which primarily provides consumer protection services. 17202. Notwithstanding Section 3369 of the Civil Code, specific or preventive relief may be granted to enforce a penalty, forfeiture, or penal law in a case of unfair competition. 17203. Injunctive Relief--Court Orders Any person who engages, has engaged, or proposes to engage in unfair competition may be enjoined in any court of competent jurisdiction. The court may make such orders or judgments, including the appointment of a receiver, as may be necessary to prevent the use or employment by any person of any practice which constitutes unfair competition, as defined in this chapter, or as may be necessary to restore to any person in interest any money or proper
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandated an "adequately trained sales organization," a clear obligation. The modified text replaces this with "endeavor to maintain a sales organization," which weakens the obligation and introduces ambiguity. California Business and Professions Code § 17200, as shown in both scraped snippets, defines unfair competition as including any "unfair or fraudulent business act or practice and unfair, deceptive, untrue or misleading advertising." A distributor who only 'endeavors' to maintain a sales organization without ensuring adequate training could engage in unfair practices due to lack of proper training, directly contradicting the law. The weaker language makes proving negligence difficult, as 'endeavoring' doesn't impose the same legal responsibility as a defined obligation, thus making it unenforceable and creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Distributor will employ sufficient trained personnel ("Application Engineers") to provide technical and marketing support for Tripath's Products. Distributor and Tripath will cooperate to ensure that the Application Engineers are and remain adequately trained and qualified.
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor will employ personnel to provide some technical and marketing support for Tripath's Products. Distributor and Tripath may cooperate to ensure that the personnel are trained.
perturbation.explanation: The words "sufficient trained" were replaced with "personnel" to remove the emphasis on adequate training, and the strong obligations were replaced with "some technical and marketing support" and "may cooperate to ensure that the personnel are trained" which create ambiguity about the distributor's actual commitment to providing sufficient technical support and ensuring adequate training of their personnel. The distributor can simply 'provide some support' and the training is subject to whether the companies decide to cooperate on it.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 2314 implies a warranty of merchantability which includes that goods are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used. By ambiguously stating the distributor's responsibilities for technical and marketing support and by not ensuring its application engineers are 'adequately trained', the distributor risks inadequately supporting Tripath's products, potentially leading to improper use and failures, thus violating implied warranty of merchantability.

SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2314. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) Pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) In the case of fungible goods, are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) Are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) Run, within the variations permitted by the agreement, of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) Are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) Conform to the promises or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. (Enacted
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the distributor's obligations regarding technical support and training, potentially leading to inadequate support for Tripath's products. The original text requires the distributor to employ "sufficient trained personnel" and cooperate to ensure they are "adequately trained and qualified." The modified text uses weaker language, stating the distributor will employ "personnel to provide some technical and marketing support" and "may cooperate to ensure that the personnel are trained." This creates ambiguity about the distributor's commitment to providing sufficient support and ensuring adequate training. According to California Commercial Code § 2314 (Snippet 1), goods must be "fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used." By weakening the requirements for technical support and training, the distributor increases the risk that Tripath's products will not be adequately supported, potentially leading to improper use and failures, thereby contradicting the implied warranty of merchantability outlined in the law.

perturbation.original_text: Distributor will maintain sufficient inventory of Products (but not less than one month's inventory without Tripath's approval) to provide prompt delivery to Distributor's customers, in furtherance of Distributor's undertaking in Subsection 2.2.
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor will attempt to maintain inventory of Products to facilitate delivery to Distributor's customers.
perturbation.explanation: The changed text is ambiguous regarding the quantity of products in inventory. The phrase "sufficient inventory" was removed and replaced with "attempt to maintain inventory", and the explicit minimum inventory requirement of one month's supply was removed. This contradicts California's laws regarding good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 1201 defines "good faith" as honesty in fact and the observance of reasonable commercial standards of fair dealing. By removing the 'sufficient inventory' and minimum inventory requirement clause and replacing it with 'attempt to maintain', the distributor's obligation to fulfill orders promptly is weakened, which could result in delays and potentially violate the good faith requirement for fair dealing in the market. A good-faith purchaser will not reasonably expect a seller to attempt, but to actually carry out their end of the deal.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation [1201 - 1206] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 1201. (a) Unless the context otherwise requires, words or phrases defined in this section, or in the additional definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof, have the meanings stated. (b) Subject to definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof: (1) “Action,” in the sense of a judicial proceeding, includes recoupment, counterclaim, setoff, suit in equity, and any other proceeding in which rights are determined. (2) “Aggrieved party” means a party entitled to pursue a remedy. (3) “Agreement,” as distinguished from “contract,” means the bargain of the parties in fact, as found in their language or inferred from other circumstances, including course of performance, course of dealing, or usage of trade as provided in Section 1303. (4) “Bank” means a person engaged in the business of banking, and includes a savings bank, savings and loan association, credit union, and trust company. (5) “Bearer” means a person in possessi
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: CHAPTER 2. GENERAL DEFINITIONS AND PRINCIPLES OF INTERPRETATION - Sections 1201-1210 :: California Commercial Code :: 2005 California Code :: California Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the California Code 2024 California Code 2023 California Code 2022 California Code 2005 California Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2005 California Commercial Code Sections 1201-1210 CHAPTER 2. GENERAL DEFINITIONS AND PRINCIPLES OF INTERPRETATION COMMERCIAL CODE SECTION 1201-1210 1201. The following definitions apply for purposes of this code, subject to additional definitions contained in the subsequent divisions of this code that apply to specific divisions or chapters thereof, and unless the context otherwise requires: (1) "Action," in the sense of a judicial proceeding, includes recoupment, counterclaim, setoff, suit in equity, and any other proceedings in which rights are determined. (2) "Aggrieved party" means a party entitled to resort to a remedy. (3) "Agreement" means the bargain of the parties in fact as found in their language or by implication from other circumstances, including course of dealing, usage of trade, and course of performance as provided in this code (Sections 1205, 2208, and 10207). Whether an agreement has legal consequences is determined by the provisions of this code, if applicable, and otherwise by the law of contracts (Section 1103). (Compare "contract.") (4) "Bank" means any person engaged in the business of banking. (5) "Bearer" means the person in possession of an instrument, document of title, or certificated security payable to bearer or endorsed in blank. (6) "Bill of lading" means a document evidencing the receipt of goods for shipment issued by a person engaged in the business of transporting or forwarding goods, and that, by its terms, evidences the intention of the issuer that the person entitled under the document (Section 7403(4)) has the right to receive, hold, and dispose of the document and the goods it covers. perturbation.justification: The original contract mandated the distributor to maintain 'sufficient inventory' with a minimum of 'one month's inventory'. The changed text replaces this with an ambiguous obligation to 'attempt to maintain inventory'. This weakening of the obligation contradicts the principle of good faith and fair dealing, as the distributor's commitment to fulfill orders is significantly reduced. The 'Law Explanation' generated by the LLM supports that a good faith purchaser would expect the seller to actually carry out their end of the deal, instead of just attempting to do so. Although the provided law snippets do not explicitly define the term 'good faith', California Commercial Code § 1201(a) states that definitions may be 'contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof'. Therefore, the removal of the specific inventory obligations introduces ambiguity making the obligation difficult to enforce.
